source_paper_title: Robustness of Stochastic Optimal Control to Approximate Diffusion Models Under Several Cost Evaluation Criteria
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Pradhan_2024, title={Robustness of Stochastic Optimal Control to Approximate Diffusion Models Under Several Cost Evaluation Criteria}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0134}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0134}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Pradhan, Somnath and Yüksel, Serdar}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2049–2077} }
context:
Page 31, Section 8 (Conclusion)

It would be an interesting problem to investigate if such results can be proved in the cases when the limiting system (actual system) is a degenerate diffusion system.
title: Extend robustness and continuity results to degenerate controlled diffusion models
background_latex:
Consider a controlled diffusion process 
\[dX_t = b(X_t,U_t)\,dt + \sigma(X_t)\,dW_t, \qquad X_0=x\in\mathbb{R}^d,\]
where \(W\) is a \(d\)-dimensional standard Wiener process, the action space \(U\) is a compact metric space, and \(U_t\) is an admissible (non-anticipative) control. The running cost is a bounded continuous function \(c:\mathbb{R}^d\times U\to\mathbb{R}_+\). For each control policy \(\pi\) (e.g., Markov or stationary Markov controls), one considers standard performance criteria such as:

1) Discounted cost for \(\alpha>0\):
\[J_\alpha^{\pi}(x;c)=\mathbb{E}^{\pi}_x\Big[\int_0^\infty e^{-\alpha t} c(X_t,U_t)\,dt\Big],\qquad V_\alpha(x)=\inf_{\pi} J_\alpha^{\pi}(x;c).\]

2) Ergodic (long-run average) cost:
\[\mathcal{E}_x(c,\pi)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi}_x\Big[\int_0^T c(X_t,U_t)\,dt\Big],\qquad \mathcal{E}^*(c)=\inf_x\inf_{\pi}\mathcal{E}_x(c,\pi).\]

3) Finite-horizon cost for a horizon \(T>0\) and terminal cost \(H\):
\[J_T^{\pi}(x;c)=\mathbb{E}^{\pi}_x\Big[\int_0^T c(X_t,U_t)\,dt + H(X_T)\Big],\qquad J_T^*(x;c)=\inf_{\pi}J_T^{\pi}(x;c).\]

The paper studies an approximation setting in which a sequence of models \((b_n,\sigma_n,c_n)\) converges to \((b,\sigma,c)\) (in a sense ensuring pointwise convergence in the state and continuous convergence in control actions), with associated state processes
\[dX_t^{(n)} = b_n(X_t^{(n)},U_t)\,dt + \sigma_n(X_t^{(n)})\,dW_t.
\]
For each model one has the corresponding optimal values \(V_{\alpha}^{(n)}\), \(\mathcal{E}^{*(n)}(c_n)\), \(J_{T}^{*(n)}(x;c_n)\), and one may compare the performance of controls optimized for the approximate model when applied to the true model.

A key standing condition used throughout the paper is uniform nondegeneracy (uniform ellipticity) of the diffusion term: letting \(a(x)=\tfrac12\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\), one assumes that for each radius \(R>0\) there exists \(C_R>0\) such that
\[z^\top a(x) z \ge C_R^{-1}\,|z|^2 \qquad \forall x\in B_R,\ \forall z\in\mathbb{R}^d,\]
(and similarly for \(a_n\)). This nondegeneracy assumption enables strong regularity theory for the associated Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman (HJB) equations and is central to the continuity/robustness proofs in the paper.

A diffusion is called degenerate if the corresponding covariance matrix \(a(x)\) fails to be uniformly positive definite (e.g., is singular in some directions, at some states, or on sets of positive measure).
problem_definition_latex:
Develop an analogue of the paper's continuity and robustness theory for controlled diffusion models when the limiting (true) diffusion is degenerate, i.e., when the uniform nondegeneracy/ellipticity condition on \(a(x)=\tfrac12\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\) is dropped and \(a(x)\) may be singular.

Concretely: under a suitable convergence notion for approximating models \((b_n,\sigma_n,c_n)\to (b,\sigma,c)\), determine conditions under which (for one or more of the criteria discounted, ergodic, finite-horizon, or exit-time) the optimal values of the approximate models converge to the optimal value of the true (degenerate) model, and the performance loss from applying an optimal control of the approximate model to the true model vanishes in the limit.
latex:
Consider a controlled diffusion process 
\[dX_t = b(X_t,U_t)\,dt + \sigma(X_t)\,dW_t, \qquad X_0=x\in\mathbb{R}^d,\]
where \(W\) is a \(d\)-dimensional standard Wiener process, the action space \(U\) is a compact metric space, and \(U_t\) is an admissible (non-anticipative) control. The running cost is a bounded continuous function \(c:\mathbb{R}^d\times U\to\mathbb{R}_+\). For each control policy \(\pi\) (e.g., Markov or stationary Markov controls), one considers standard performance criteria such as:

1) Discounted cost for \(\alpha>0\):
\[J_\alpha^{\pi}(x;c)=\mathbb{E}^{\pi}_x\Big[\int_0^\infty e^{-\alpha t} c(X_t,U_t)\,dt\Big],\qquad V_\alpha(x)=\inf_{\pi} J_\alpha^{\pi}(x;c).\]

2) Ergodic (long-run average) cost:
\[\mathcal{E}_x(c,\pi)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}^{\pi}_x\Big[\int_0^T c(X_t,U_t)\,dt\Big],\qquad \mathcal{E}^*(c)=\inf_x\inf_{\pi}\mathcal{E}_x(c,\pi).\]

3) Finite-horizon cost for a horizon \(T>0\) and terminal cost \(H\):
\[J_T^{\pi}(x;c)=\mathbb{E}^{\pi}_x\Big[\int_0^T c(X_t,U_t)\,dt + H(X_T)\Big],\qquad J_T^*(x;c)=\inf_{\pi}J_T^{\pi}(x;c).\]

\[dX_t^{(n)} = b_n(X_t^{(n)},U_t)\,dt + \sigma_n(X_t^{(n)})\,dW_t.
\]
For each model one has the corresponding optimal values \(V_{\alpha}^{(n)}\), \(\mathcal{E}^{*(n)}(c_n)\), \(J_{T}^{*(n)}(x;c_n)\), and one may compare the performance of controls optimized for the approximate model when applied to the true model.

A key standing condition used throughout the paper is uniform nondegeneracy (uniform ellipticity) of the diffusion term: letting \(a(x)=\tfrac12\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\), one assumes that for each radius \(R>0\) there exists \(C_R>0\) such that
\[z^\top a(x) z \ge C_R^{-1}\,|z|^2 \qquad \forall x\in B_R,\ \forall z\in\mathbb{R}^d,\]
(and similarly for \(a_n\)). This nondegeneracy assumption enables strong regularity theory for the associated Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman (HJB) equations and is central to the continuity/robustness proofs in the paper.

A diffusion is called degenerate if the corresponding covariance matrix \(a(x)\) fails to be uniformly positive definite (e.g., is singular in some directions, at some states, or on sets of positive measure).

Develop an analogue of the paper's continuity and robustness theory for controlled diffusion models when the limiting (true) diffusion is degenerate, i.e., when the uniform nondegeneracy/ellipticity condition on \(a(x)=\tfrac12\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\) is dropped and \(a(x)\) may be singular.

Concretely: under a suitable convergence notion for approximating models \((b_n,\sigma_n,c_n)\to (b,\sigma,c)\), determine conditions under which (for one or more of the criteria discounted, ergodic, finite-horizon, or exit-time) the optimal values of the approximate models converge to the optimal value of the true (degenerate) model, and the performance loss from applying an optimal control of the approximate model to the true model vanishes in the limit.
open_problem_quote: It would be an interesting problem to investigate if such results can be proved in the cases when the limiting system (actual system) is a degenerate diffusion system.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is broadly about extending positive results to a substantially different analytical regime (loss of uniform ellipticity), rather than a sharp finite-dimensional conjecture. Searching for counterexamples is possible in principle, but the space of degenerate diffusions and cost criteria is huge and failure modes may depend on subtle analytic details.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize families of degenerate diffusion-control problems (e.g., low-dimensional SDEs with rank-deficient \(\sigma\)) and numerically test continuity/robustness across approximations by solving HJB equations or simulating candidate policies. However, the main bottleneck is formulating a faithful computable proxy for the universal convergence statements and ensuring numerical PDE/Monte Carlo methods remain stable in degenerate settings.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Pradhan--Y\"uksel (arXiv:2205.05894v2, 2023) develop a PDE-based continuity/robustness theory for controlled diffusions under discounted, ergodic (near-monotone or Lyapunov), finite-horizon, and exit-time criteria. A central technical pillar is local uniform ellipticity (Assumption (A3)), which yields Sobolev/Schauder regularity for HJB equations, compactness of value-function families in \(W^{2,p}\), and It\^o--Krylov representations enabling both value convergence \(V^n\to V\) and asymptotic optimality transfer when applying \(n\)-optimal controls to the limit model.

Forward-citing work to date provides partial progress but does not remove ellipticity. The regime-switching extension proves analogous continuity/robustness results for weakly coupled HJB systems, again under local uniform ellipticity, and thus mainly maps where ellipticity enters and what would need replacement in a degenerate setting. The rough-path robustness paper proves policy-wise continuity and near-optimality transfer for Lipschitz feedback controls under near-Brownian noise perturbations; while it still assumes uniform nondegeneracy to access classical HJB theory for the idealized model, its rough-path continuity arguments are largely independent of ellipticity and suggest a promising tool for establishing continuity of \(J^\pi\) for fixed (regular) feedbacks even when \(\sigma\) is degenerate.

Overall, the open problem remains to develop a full analogue of Pradhan--Y\"uksel robustness when the limiting diffusion is degenerate: one needs conditions ensuring existence/approximation of optimal controls and convergence of optimal values without relying on uniformly elliptic PDE regularity or strong Feller properties. Promising directions include (i) viscosity-solution stability for degenerate HJB equations combined with measurable selection/relaxed controls, (ii) hypoelliptic regularity under H\"ormander-type conditions when degeneracy is structured, and (iii) probabilistic/MDP discretization approaches (e.g., Wasserstein-regular kernel continuity) that bypass elliptic PDE estimates.

sources.title: Robustness of optimal controlled diffusions with near-brownian noise via rough paths theory
sources.theorem_or_result:
Under assumptions (A1)–(A4), including uniform nondegeneracy (A3), and restricting to Lipschitz Markov (or Lipschitz time-state) controls, the paper proves robustness of optimal control to near-Brownian noise perturbations in rough-path topology: if \(v_\delta\) is a Lipschitz \(\delta\)-optimal control for the Brownian/Stratonovich idealized model, then for sufficiently small \(\varepsilon\) (or large \(n\) in explicit approximations) one has
- Discounted cost: \(J^{v_\delta}_{\alpha,T_\varepsilon}(x,c) \le V^{L T_\varepsilon}_\alpha(x)+2\delta\) (Theorem 5.4 / 5.9).
- Finite horizon: \(J^{v_\delta}_{T,T_\varepsilon}(x,c) \le J^{*}_{T,L T_\varepsilon}(x,c)+2\delta\) (Theorem 5.8 / 5.10).
It also establishes continuity of costs for fixed Lipschitz policies (Proposition 5.1, Lemma 5.5) and convergence of restricted value functions \(V^{L T_\varepsilon}_\alpha\to V^I_\alpha\), \(J^{*}_{T,L T_\varepsilon}\to J^{*}_{T,I}\) up to \(\delta\) (Theorems 5.3, 5.7).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks to extend continuity/robustness results to degenerate controlled diffusions (dropping uniform ellipticity). This citing paper is closely related in spirit—it studies robustness/continuity of optimal control under model mismatch—but it does not remove ellipticity. In fact it explicitly assumes uniform nondegeneracy (A3) and uses classical HJB/PDE regularity (Theorems 2.1–2.2) to justify existence/near-optimality of Lipschitz controls for the idealized diffusion. Rough paths are then used to obtain pathwise continuity of the state trajectory with respect to perturbations of the driving noise (and drift), yielding convergence of costs for fixed Lipschitz policies and near-optimality transfer.

Thus, it does not solve the degenerate-diffusion extension. However, it provides a potentially relevant methodological ingredient for the degenerate setting: the rough-path continuity arguments (Theorem 3.9, Corollary 4.6) are largely independent of ellipticity and could still yield continuity of \(J^\pi\) for fixed Lipschitz feedbacks even when \(\sigma\) is degenerate. What remains open (and is not addressed here) is the control-optimization layer in the degenerate case: existence/approximation of optimal controls and convergence of optimal values without relying on uniformly elliptic HJB regularity/strong Feller properties.

sources.title: Robustness of optimal control for controlled regime-switching diffusions with incorrect models
sources.theorem_or_result: Under local Lipschitz and growth assumptions plus a local uniform ellipticity (nondegeneracy) condition (A3) for both the true and approximating models, the paper proves continuity of optimal value functions and robustness of optimal controls for controlled regime-switching diffusions under four criteria: discounted, ergodic (with a Foster–Lyapunov stability condition (A7)), finite-horizon, and exit-time. Concretely, if \((b_n,\sigma_n,c_n,m_n)\to(b,\sigma,c,m)\) as in (A6), then \(V^n_\alpha(x,i)\to V_\alpha(x,i)\) (Thm 3.3), \(\mathcal E^{n*}(c_n)=\rho_n\to\rho=\mathcal E^*(c)\) (Thm 4.3), \(J^*_{T,n}(x,i;c_n)\to J^*_T(x,i;c)\) (Thm 5.3), and \(\hat J^{*}_{e,n}(x,i)\to \hat J^{*}_e(x,i)\) (Thm 6.3); moreover applying an optimal control from the \(n\)-model to the true model yields asymptotically optimal performance (Thms 3.4, 4.5, 5.4, 6.4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to extend robustness/continuity results to degenerate controlled diffusions where the limiting diffusion matrix \(a(x)=\tfrac12\sigma\sigma^\top\) may be singular (dropping uniform ellipticity). This paper does not address degeneracy: it explicitly assumes local uniform nondegeneracy (A3) for the true regime-switching diffusion and for every approximating model (A6)(iii). The proofs rely on elliptic/parabolic Sobolev estimates, ABP/Harnack-type bounds, and It\^o–Krylov formula for \(W^{2,p}\) solutions of (weakly coupled) HJB systems—tools that typically require (at least local) uniform ellipticity. Thus it does not solve the degenerate-diffusion extension, but it provides a closely related robustness framework (including exit-time and hybrid switching) whose arguments indicate exactly where ellipticity is used and what would need replacement (e.g., hypoelliptic/degenerate PDE regularity or viscosity-solution stability).

sources.title: Robustness to Model Approximation, Model Learning From Data, and Sample Complexity in Wasserstein Regular MDPs
sources.theorem_or_result: For discrete-time MDPs on Polish state spaces with compact action spaces, the paper proves quantitative continuity and robustness bounds for optimal value functions and for the performance loss of applying an optimal policy from an approximate model to the true model, with bounds controlled by \,\(\|c-\hat c\|_\infty\) and a uniform Wasserstein-1 discrepancy \,\(d_{W_1}(T,S)=\sup_{x,u} W_1(T(\cdot\mid x,u),S(\cdot\mid x,u))\). Representative results include: (i) discounted-cost continuity (Theorem 2.4) \(\|J^*_\beta(c,T)-J^*_\beta(\hat c,S)\|_\infty\le \frac{1}{1-\beta}\|c-\hat c\|_\infty+\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\,d_{J^*_\beta(c,T)}(T,S)\), and if \(J^*_\beta(c,T)\) is Lipschitz then \(\le \frac{1}{1-\beta}\|c-\hat c\|_\infty+\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\|J^*_\beta(c,T)\|_{\mathrm{Lip}} d_{W_1}(T,S)\); (ii) discounted robustness error bounds (Theorem 2.7) of order \(\frac{1}{1-\beta}\|c-\hat c\|_\infty+\frac{\beta}{1-\beta} d_{J^*}(T,S)\); (iii) analogous average-cost continuity/robustness bounds under either a minorization condition (Theorems 2.5, 2.8) or a vanishing-discount approach under Wasserstein contraction/Lipschitz assumptions (Theorems 2.6, 2.9).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for robustness/continuity of optimal control for continuous-time controlled diffusions when the limiting diffusion is degenerate (no uniform ellipticity). This paper does not treat continuous-time diffusions or HJB/PDE regularity; it works in discrete time with general MDP kernels and uses Wasserstein-1 regularity/minorization/vanishing-discount arguments. However, its methodology is relevant as a potential alternative route for degenerate diffusions: one could discretize time (Euler–Maruyama) to obtain MDP kernels and then seek Wasserstein-type continuity of those kernels under model approximation without relying on elliptic PDE regularity. The paper thus provides tools/benchmarks for robustness bounds in a setting where nondegeneracy is not assumed, but it does not directly resolve the diffusion-degenerate continuous-time robustness question.
